items.why: Students reverse IV and DV. The IV is what you CHANGE (input); the DV is what you MEASURE (outcome). Mnemonic: 'I change the I.'

items.correct: Every member of the target population has an equal chance of selection.
items.misconception: Just grabbing whoever is around (any haphazard method).
items.random_wrong: Picking participants who match the population profile.
items.why: Students treat 'random' as 'casual' or 'haphazard'. True randomness requires a defined sampling frame and a chance procedure (e.g., random number generator).

items.q: Why does the cognitive approach use INFERENCE?
items.correct: Mental processes cannot be directly observed, so they are inferred from behaviour.
items.misconception: Because cognitive psychologists guess instead of measuring.
items.why: Inference is a methodological necessity, not a weakness of rigour. Students treat it as vague guessing. It means drawing conclusions about unobservable processes from observable behaviour.

items.q: Which sleep stage features rapid eye movements and vivid dreams?
items.why: Correct answer: REM. “Stage 1 NREM” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Activation-synthesis theory of dreaming was proposed by:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Hobson and McCarley. “Freud” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately how many hours is the circadian rhythm cycle?
items.why: The real figure is 24 hours. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
